Description: The average number of seconds per defensive chance. Description: Individual defensive player rating (iDRtg) is an all-in-one metric developed by Dean Oliver in his 2004 book Basketball on Paper. In Oliver's words, individual defensive rating estimates how many points the player allowed per 100 possessions he individually faced while on the court. iDRtg is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for iDRtg can be found at https://www.basketball-reference.com/about/ratings.html
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Defensive RAPM (DRAPM) is an all-in-one metric we've developed internally here at CBB Analytics. DRAPM incorporates both a player's individual box score statistics as well as their on-court play-by-play impact, which means DRAPM is able to account for who the player is playing with and against. DRAPM can be interpreted as a player's impact on their team's points allowed per 100 possessions below the division average. DRAPM is similar to the defensive components of all-in-one statistics developed for the NBA such as RPM, EPM, and LEBRON.
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Defensive Win Shares (DWS) is an all-in-one metric originally developed by Bill James for baseball, and adapted by Justin Kubatko (basketball-reference) for use in basketball. DWS attempts to divvy up credit for team success on defense to the individuals on the team, by estimating the number of defensive possessions a player was involved in over the year and the number of points prevented by the player's defensive efforts, which are then converted into the number of wins the player has contributed to their team. DWS is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for DWS can be found at https://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/about/ws.html
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Individual offensive player rating (iORtg) is an all-in-one metric developed by Dean Oliver in his 2004 book Basketball on Paper. In Oliver's words, individual offensive rating is the number of points produced by a player per hundred total individual possessions, or in other words, how many points a player is likely to generate when he tries. iORtg is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for iORtg can be found at https://www.basketball-reference.com/about/ratings.html
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Offensive RAPM (ORAPM) is an all-in-one metric we've developed internally here at CBB Analytics. ORAPM incorporates both a player's individual box score statistics as well as their on-court play-by-play impact, which means ORAPM is able to account for who the player is playing with and against. ORAPM can be interpreted as a player's impact on their team's points scored per 100 possessions above the division average. ORAPM is similar to the offensive components of all-in-one statistics developed for the NBA such as RPM, EPM, and LEBRON.
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Points scored per 100 possessions by the team.
Available In: Team Game, Team Agg, Lineup, On/Off

Description: Offensive Win Shares (OWS) is an all-in-one metric originally developed by Bill James for baseball, and adapted by Justin Kubatko (basketball-reference) for use in basketball. OWS attempts to divvy up credit for team success on offense to the individuals on the team, by estimating the number of possessions a player used over the year and the number of points produced from those possessions, which are then converted into the number of wins the player has contributed to their team. OWS is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for OWS can be found at https://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/about/ws.html
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Player efficiency rating (PER) is an all-in-one metric developed by John Hollinger. In Hollinger's words, PER sums up all of a player's positive accomplishments, subtracts the negative accomplishments, and returns a per-minute rating of a player's performance. PER is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. PER measures offensive performance more so than it does defensive performance. The equation for PER can be found at https://www.basketball-reference.com/about/per.html

Description: RAPM (Regularized Adjusted Plus/Minus) is an all-in-one metric we've developed internally here at CBB Analytics. RAPM does not incorporate a player's individual box score statistics, rather it only considers their on-court play-by-play impact. RAPM can be interpreted as a player's impact on their team's point differential per 100 possessions above the division average, accounting for the other 9 players on the court. RAPM is similar to all-in-one statistics developed for the NBA such as RPM, EPM, and LEBRON.

Description: Value Point System (VPS) is an all-in-one metric developed by Hall of Fame Coach Danny Miles. In Miles' words, VPS provides an all-encompassing look at how players and teams perform by weighing their positive contributions against their negative ones - the resulting number shows how effective the player is. A VPS of 1 is considered average, while anything north of 2 is elite. VPS  is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against.
Available In: Player Agg, Player Game

Description: Win Shares (WS) is an all-in-one metric originally developed by Bill James for baseball, and adapted by Justin Kubatko (basketball-reference) for use in basketball. WS attempts to divvy up credit for team success to the individuals on the team, encompassing both offensive and defensive contributions. WS is the sum of a player's Offensive Win Shares (OWS) and Defensive Win Shares (DWS), providing a comprehensive estimate of the total number of wins a player has contributed to their team. WS is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for DWS can be found at https://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/about/ws.html
Available In: Player Agg

Description: Wins Above Replacement Player (WARP) is an all-in-one metric developed by Kevin Pelton. In Pelton's words, WARP seeks to evaluate players in the context of a team made up of them and four completely average players. The performance of this team is then compared to that of a team made up of four average players and one replacement-level player. WARP is computed using box score statistics only, and therefore does not account for who the player is playing with or against. The equation for WARP can be found at https://sonicscentral.com/warp.html
Available In: Player Agg
